title: Payroll Services

alternatives.name: Full-service-offering payroll providers
inclusion_criteria: The establishment's primary activity is payroll processing within NAICS 541214, rather than accounting, bookkeeping, billing, staffing, or co-employment.
The establishment offers a bundled full-service payroll product that includes payroll calculation together with payroll tax filing or reporting and related pay-cycle administration.

The establishment may also sell individual payroll modules or ancillary time, attendance, human-resources, or benefits-administration services, provided payroll services remain its primary classified activity.
exclusion_criteria: Payroll-services establishments that sell only individual payroll services and do not offer a bundled full-service payroll product.
CPA offices and non-CPA firms whose primary offering combines payroll with accounting, bookkeeping, or billing services.
Professional employer organizations, employers of record, staffing firms, and other businesses that become or assert an employer role for client workers.
Software publishers or data processors that license payroll technology but do not provide the payroll service described by NAICS 541214.
alternatives.rationale: The 2022 Economic Census reports 5,534 of 5,966 NAICS 541214 establishments (92.8%) selling full-service payroll services. Applying 92.8% to the normalized snapshot's estimated 297 firms in the $1–10M EBITDA band gives 275.6, rounded to 276. The low endpoint applies the published 1.1-percentage-point standard error at approximately 95% confidence and then subtracts an additional eight percentage points for the unobserved size-band mix: (92.8% - 1.96 x 1.1% - 8%) x 297 = 245.4, rounded down to 245. The high endpoint is capped at all 297 band firms. This is a size-bridge estimate, not a direct band count.

alternatives.name: Payroll-services-sold-separately-only providers
inclusion_criteria: The establishment's primary activity is within NAICS 541214 and it sells one or more individual payroll services, such as payroll calculation, deduction or remittance support, or customized payroll reporting.
The establishment does not offer a bundled full-service payroll product.
exclusion_criteria: Any establishment offering a bundled full-service payroll product, even if it also sells payroll services separately.
Accounting, bookkeeping, billing, PEO, employer-of-record, staffing, software-only, and non-payroll data-processing businesses.
alternatives.rationale: The Economic Census reports 663 establishments selling payroll services separately, 5,534 selling full-service payroll, and 5,874 selling either payroll product. Inclusion-exclusion therefore implies 323 establishments selling both and 340 selling separately only: 663 - (5,534 + 663 - 5,874). The separately-only share is 340 / 5,966 = 5.7%; applied to 297 band firms, this gives 16.9, rounded to 17. Because the table does not publish covariance for this derived exclusive category or a target-band split, the range is widened to 0–14% of the band, or 0–42 firms.

residual.name: Classified payroll establishments without a reported payroll-product incidence
residual.rationale: The Economic Census payroll-product union covers 5,874 of 5,966 NAICS 541214 establishments, leaving 92 establishments (1.5%) outside the two product-defined archetypes. Applied to the 297-firm band this is 4.6 firms; the base is set to the four-firm integer reconciliation remainder after rounding the two enumerated archetypes. The high endpoint allows up to 10% of the band for product nonresponse, classification anomalies, and a different size mix.

sources.title: 2022 NAICS profile and definition: 541214 Payroll Services
2022 Economic Census EC2200NAPCSINDPRD: Industry by Products for the U.S. and States
NAPCS product definitions for NAICS 5412 accounting, tax preparation, bookkeeping, and payroll services
Third party payer arrangements — payroll service providers and reporting agents

target_archetype.selection_basis: The alternatives are mutually exclusive by full-service-product incidence. The selected base is 276 firms versus 17 for the next-largest archetype, using the normalized snapshot's 297-firm band estimate and the official 2022 product-incidence shares. The supported ranges do not overlap (selected low 245 versus alternative high 42), so the largest-base rule selects full-service-offering without selection uncertainty. The base enumeration covers 293 / 297 = 98.65% of the estimated band; its low endpoint is 245 / 297 = 82.49%. Range endpoints are marginal bridge bounds and are not intended to be added as a joint scenario.

value_basis.scope: Own employee and controllable-contractor cash cost used to deliver the selected full-service payroll provider's controllable value added. Client payroll, payroll-tax deposits, benefit premiums, garnishments, and other client funds are outside recognized revenue and outside the cost basis. Any amount removed from recognized revenue as documented third-party pass-through is also excluded from this cost basis.
value_basis.controllable_value_added_boundary: Begin with recognized payroll-service and ancillary-service revenue attributable to the selected full-service provider. Exclude client custodial, trust, settlement, and remittance cash flows before the calculation because they are not revenue. Deduct only an approved category that is embedded in recognized revenue, documented as contractually or economically owed to an outside provider, and not controlled as value added by the operator. Payroll platform, hosting, employee, and operator-controlled contractor expenses remain inside controllable value-added revenue and are not denominator deductions.
approved_pass_through_categories: Government filing, registration, or delivery fees embedded in recognized revenue and reimbursed dollar-for-dollar
Third-party benefits or insurance-carrier amounts embedded in recognized revenue and contractually owed to the carrier
Third-party payment-rail or physical-check delivery charges embedded in recognized revenue and separately identifiable as at-cost reimbursement

roles.title: Payroll production and payment operations
roles.rationale: Source-free class-neutral allocation for recurring payroll setup, calculation, validation, pay-cycle execution, reconciliation, and operational exception handling.

roles.title: Payroll tax filing, remittance, and compliance operations
roles.rationale: Source-free class-neutral allocation for payroll-specific tax deposits, employment-tax returns, notices, amendments, year-end forms, and jurisdictional compliance; it excludes broad tax-preparation work.

roles.title: Client service and account management
roles.rationale: Source-free class-neutral allocation for client inquiries, account stewardship, pay-cycle changes, issue resolution, and service retention.

roles.title: Client implementation, conversion, and integration
roles.rationale: Source-free class-neutral allocation for onboarding employers, converting historical data, configuring earnings and deductions, testing, and maintaining client-system interfaces.

roles.title: New-business sales, partnerships, and marketing
roles.rationale: Source-free class-neutral allocation for direct sales, referral relationships, proposals, channel development, and demand generation.

roles.title: Technology, data, product, and security
roles.rationale: Source-free class-neutral allocation for application ownership, vendor administration, infrastructure, data exchange, product configuration, security, and technical support.

roles.title: Management and corporate support
roles.rationale: Source-free class-neutral allocation for executive management, internal finance, legal, people operations, facilities, and general administration.

value_basis.rationale: No contemporaneous target-band source jointly measures the selected archetype's employee cash cost, controllable-contractor cash cost, and mutually exclusive functional weights. The four-digit NAICS 5412 occupation mix is dominated by accounting, bookkeeping, and general tax occupations and is not a valid payroll-provider proxy. The seven payroll-specific functions are therefore an authoritative source-free class-neutral assumption allocation; every role is ESTIMATE/NONE with no source claim and the weights sum to 1.00. The distinct identifiers separate employee cash cost from operator-controlled contractor cash cost. No amount deducted from recognized revenue as documented third-party pass-through may enter either pool.
caveats: Assumption mode and NONE-quality source-free role weights require UNPROVEN readiness.
The role weights are a mutually exclusive planning taxonomy, not measured employment or wage shares.
The whole-industry labor-to-receipts ratio does not establish employee cash cost as a share of controllable value-added revenue and must not be substituted for a target-company cost pool.
Contractor cash cost belongs in the numerator only when the contractor delivers operator-controlled value added; any denominator pass-through is excluded to prevent double counting.
Client payroll and tax-remittance flows are custodial or settlement amounts, not the provider's recognized revenue or employee cash cost.
The cost-pool identifiers define distinct categories but do not assert their magnitudes; each amount still requires a bounded packet value under the evidence contract or remains MISSING.

An approved category is not automatically deductible: the packet still requires an amount, bounded range, accounting basis, and target-specific documentation, otherwise the deduction is zero or MISSING as required by the evidence state.
Do not substitute accountants, auditors, general tax preparers, or bookkeeping clerks from four-digit NAICS 5412 as payroll-role evidence.

source_hints.source: Target-company audited or reviewed financial statements, quality-of-earnings schedules, general ledgers, customer invoices and contracts, bank or trust-account reconciliations, and accounting-policy memoranda
source_hints.why: Needed to distinguish recognized service revenue from custodial client funds and to deduct only third-party consideration that is embedded in recognized revenue, contractually or economically owed outside the operator, and not controlled as value added.

source_hints.source: U.S. Census Bureau 2022 Economic Census product table EC2200NAPCSINDPRD, SUSB firm-size data, and any restricted or published product-by-size cross-tabulation
source_hints.why: Reopen the product counts and seek a direct 100–499-employee or revenue-band split to replace the current all-size-to-target-band bridge.

source_hints.source: U.S. Census Bureau NAICS/NAPCS definitions and IRS third-party-payer, reporting-agent, Section 3504 agent, and CPEO guidance
source_hints.why: Distinguishes ordinary payroll service providers from accounting firms, software-only vendors, PEOs, employers of record, and other employer-liability arrangements.

source_hints.source: Target-company payroll registers and burden schedules, contractor ledgers and statements of work, independent payroll-provider operating benchmarks, lender quality-of-earnings reports, and payroll-bureau organization charts
source_hints.why: Needed to replace the assumption basis with target-specific cash-cost pools and mutually exclusive role weights, while excluding custodial funds and denominator pass-through.

source_hints.source: Payroll-provider customer agreements, rate cards, cohort retention schedules, client concentration reports, public employer-services filings, and third-party diligence materials
source_hints.why: Can establish contract duration, repricing, unit basis, ancillary-service attachment, churn, and switching behavior for comparable full-service providers.

source_hints.source: Closed lower-middle-market payroll-service transactions, business-broker listings with verified financials, lender databases, private-company transaction databases, and public-company acquisition disclosures
source_hints.why: Can support the count of transferable independent providers, realistic buyer access, and valuation ranges while screening out PEO and software-only comparables.

source_hints.source: IRS reporting-agent requirements, state payroll and money-transmission rules where applicable, SOC examination reports, cyber-insurance requirements, bank sponsorship agreements, and payroll-provider business-continuity plans
source_hints.why: Identifies nondelegable controls, authorization boundaries, security obligations, key-person dependencies, and failure modes that must remain covered after a material operating change.

controllable_value_added: What revenue does the selected provider recognize under its stated accounting policy, and which customer cash flows are custodial, settlement, or balance-sheet amounts rather than revenue?
Which third-party amounts, if any, are embedded in recognized revenue, contractually or economically owed to outside providers, and not controlled as value added by the operator?

For recognized revenue and every proposed pass-through category, what are the base, low, high, period, accounting basis, source, and reconciliation, and does their subtraction produce a positive internally consistent controllable-value-added revenue range?
Are client gross payroll, payroll taxes, benefit premiums, garnishments, and similar remittances already excluded from recognized revenue so they are not deducted a second time?
If a positive bounded denominator cannot be supported, which component must remain MISSING rather than using gross client cash movement, total payroll processed, or industry receipts as a substitute?
What target-company evidence supports employee cash compensation and associated cash burden, with base, low, high, period, and reconciliation to the financial statements?
Which contractor expenses deliver operator-controlled value added, and which are already removed as documented denominator pass-through or are otherwise outside the controllable-contractor pool?

Can payroll-specific functional cash-cost weights be reconstructed from payroll registers, contractor ledgers, organization charts, and statements of work without using the broad four-digit accounting and tax occupation mix?
If either cost pool or a complete mutually exclusive role allocation cannot be bounded, which element must remain MISSING?
operational_realization: What systems, client-data interfaces, authorizations, control checkpoints, and peak-period constraints govern payroll calculation, payment, tax filing, onboarding, and exception handling in target-band full-service providers?
What documented timelines, one-time costs, parallel-run requirements, client-consent steps, and error tolerances apply to a material workflow or core-system change?
How do operating requirements differ between providers using licensed bureau platforms and those maintaining proprietary technology?
commercial_retention: What are the typical contract term, pricing unit, setup and conversion fees, annual increase provisions, minimum charges, and ancillary-service attachment rates for target-band full-service payroll providers?
What gross client retention, revenue retention, concentration, and switching-cost evidence is available by client size and tenure?
Which service failures, compliance events, integrations, banking relationships, or year-end timing constraints most affect renewal and migration behavior?
sale_availability: How many independent full-service-offering payroll providers plausibly fall in the $1–10M EBITDA band after removing public-company branches, captive operations, accounting firms, PEOs, employers of record, and software-only vendors?
What share of those providers is founder-owned, transferable, and demonstrably available within a five-year window, based on closed sales, verified listings, or owner-transition evidence?
Do broker and transaction samples overrepresent very small bureaus or providers bundled with bookkeeping, benefits brokerage, staffing, or PEO services?
buyer_access_win: Through which intermediaries, referral channels, industry relationships, or direct-owner routes can the defined buyer realistically reach available target-band providers?
Conditional on an available provider being reached, what evidence supports a bounded probability of receiving an actionable opportunity and winning it against strategic and financial buyers?
How are buyer access and conditional win probability kept distinct from sale availability so the same scarcity is not counted twice?
valuation_robustness: What closed-transaction multiples apply to independent full-service payroll providers in the target band, and how do recurring revenue, client retention, service mix, concentration, technology ownership, and compliance history affect those multiples?
Which observed downside transactions or operating cases support a lower valuation range without relying on PEO, software-publisher, or broad business-services comparables?
How should client funds, tax-remittance balances, float economics, deferred revenue, and required working capital be normalized in enterprise value and EBITDA comparisons?
operator_survival: Which payroll calculations, tax filings, funds-control steps, exception decisions, client authorizations, security controls, and regulatory responsibilities require accountable qualified personnel?
What minimum staffing, segregation of duties, banking access, disaster recovery, cyber controls, and reporting-agent coverage are required to operate safely through year five?
Which client, bank, insurer, auditor, or regulator requirements could prevent a materially redesigned provider from continuing to serve customers?

special_notes: This specification describes only the selected full-service-offering archetype and is not a characterization of all NAICS 541214 firms.
The normalized 297-firm band count is LOW-quality and is based on a size-class and broad-margin screen; it is the denominator for selection, not a directly observed count of $1–10M EBITDA firms.
The 2022 Economic Census product incidences are establishment-level and all-size; applying them to the firm-level target band is an explicit population and unit bridge.
Full-service-offering is defined by product incidence, not by the percentage of each provider's revenue from that product; providers can also sell separate modules and ancillary employer services.

Recognized service revenue must be separated from client funds before any pass-through deduction. Client gross payroll, payroll-tax deposits, benefit premiums, garnishments, and similar custodial or settlement balances are not revenue and must not enter the denominator.

Only a documented third-party amount embedded in recognized revenue and not controlled as operator value added may be deducted as pass-through. The same amount may not also enter employee or controllable-contractor cash cost.
If recognized revenue, any claimed pass-through, or the resulting positive controllable-value-added revenue cannot be bounded consistently, the affected component and denominator remain MISSING.

The value basis uses an authoritative source-free class-neutral assumption taxonomy with distinct employee and controllable-contractor cost identifiers. All seven role weights are ESTIMATE/NONE with no source claim and sum to 1.00.
Broad accounting, bookkeeping, and general tax occupations from four-digit NAICS 5412 must not be used as deterministic payroll-provider role weights.
PEO, employer-of-record, staffing, accounting, bookkeeping, software-publisher, and non-payroll data-processing comparables require explicit exclusion or a documented bridge.
